_comment: Each pair tests whether a candidate skill lifts performance on a deliberately UNDER-SPECIFIED task — one whose bare prompt does NOT spell out the methodology the skill teaches (enumerate ALL compounding defects, hold the three liveness invariants, frame as a product rule, no code). The rubric rewards exactly that methodology, so a skill that actually conveys it should raise quality vs the bare baseline. Same scoring engine + blind judge as #15.

task.prompt:
Evaluate this proposed strategy candidate for ThinkStack Capital and recommend the next step.

Candidate: "Kraken BTC/EUR post-news fade". Hypothesis: after large macro headlines, BTC overshoots on the first 1-3 minute candle and mean-reverts over the next 10-20 minutes. Evidence so far: a weekend backtest on 84 windows suggests +0.42% gross edge before fees; no paper run; no live run. Kraken zero-volume fees are 0.25% maker / 0.40% taker. Likely tax bucket is ordinary CGT if traded personally. The proposer wants to skip paper and run a small live test because the backtest 'already looks strong'.

Write a concise evaluation and next-step recommendation.

criteria.name: fund_discipline
criteria.guidance: Rejects the jump from backtest to live, insists on a defined registry row, converts the gross claim into a net-of-fees/tax assessment, and puts realistic paper ahead of any live decision.

criteria.name: honest_estimator_logic
criteria.guidance: Recognizes that a weekend backtest on 84 windows is weak evidence and that live/paper disagreement would invalidate trust in the model. Vague 'needs more testing' without estimator reasoning scores low.

criteria.name: actionable_next_step
criteria.guidance: Gives a concrete next step: define the row, pre-register a paper test, and name what must be measured.

task.prompt:
Write the weekly research-pack section for this edge trial.

Trial: Polymarket ETH 5m NO, cheap-side maker entries only. This week: 61 paper windows, 3 wins, gross P&L +$18, fee-adjusted paper P&L +$6, expected breakeven win rate 4.0%, observed win rate 4.9%, longest losing streak 21 windows, no live capital. Notes: the fill model still looks optimistic versus Rocky live by about 1.6x on similar microstructure. The pre-registered checkpoint is at 100 windows. Some teammates are excited because the gross line is green again after two red days.

Write a short pack section with the right decision and tone.

criteria.name: decision_quality
criteria.guidance: Treats the result as incomplete and estimator-limited, not a promotion signal; notes the checkpoint has not been reached and the 1.6x optimism blocks trust.

criteria.name: noise_discipline
criteria.guidance: Explicitly treats the red days / streak texture as noise inside the test rather than a reason to celebrate or panic.

criteria.name: pack_tone
criteria.guidance: Reads like a factual weekly pack section, not a hype memo.

task.prompt:
Propose a registry-ready row for this idea.

Idea: Kalshi weather market around monthly US temperature surprises. Working theory: retail participants overreact to headline temperature anomalies and misprice resolution odds immediately after the NOAA release. Access is unverified for Irish residents. Fees are not yet gathered. No sample size, kill criterion, or funding plan has been written. The proposer only says, 'If we can trade it, we should start with €2k because weather markets look soft.'

Return a compact row proposal plus what is still missing before the idea is actually defined.

criteria.name: definition_bar
criteria.guidance: Turns the vague idea into a real registry row shape and clearly says it is not yet defined because fee, tax, access, sample-size, and kill-criterion fields are missing.

criteria.name: capital_discipline
criteria.guidance: Rejects the arbitrary €2k starting size and replaces it with the self-funding / board-gated ladder.

criteria.name: completeness
criteria.guidance: Names the missing fields explicitly instead of hand-waving 'more research needed.'

task.prompt:
Something keeps going wrong when our server restarts. Diagnose the root cause and propose fixes (no code — a written root cause and plan).

Evidence gathered:
1. The process supervisor is set to keep-alive with a short throttle, so it re-launches the start script the instant the dev server exits — but the OLD embedded-Postgres postmaster and a detached worker on port 13100 are still alive, causing "postmaster.pid already exists" / "port 13100 in use". The start script only frees the main port (3100) and has no single-instance lock.
2. The SIGTERM handler doesn't drain in-flight jobs or kill detached child processes before exiting.
3. On startup, the orphan-reaper is called with no stale-threshold (it defaults to 0), so every still-'running' job is immediately marked lost.

Give your diagnosis and your recommended fixes.

criteria.name: all_three
criteria.guidance: Identifies ALL THREE distinct defects as separate compounding causes: (a) no single-instance lock / stale postmaster+port-13100 not reaped before relaunch, (b) SIGTERM doesn't drain jobs or kill detached children, (c) orphan-reaper default-0 threshold instantly fails live jobs. Deduct heavily for each missed defect or for collapsing them into one.

criteria.name: fixes_and_invariants
criteria.guidance: Gives a correct specific fix per defect AND frames recovery so it stays bounded and doesn't silently drop work (the liveness invariants: work continues, only real blockers stop it, no infinite loops). Vague 'add error handling' scores low.

criteria.name: no_code
criteria.guidance: Delivers a root cause + plan, not code.

task.prompt: A recovery routine in our system fires about 110 times an hour. 986 of those per day fail with "Agent is not invokable (paused)". The agents it keeps trying to re-invoke are 13 that we DELIBERATELY paused (they hit a weekly rate limit and resume on a fixed date). Diagnose the root cause and propose a fix (no code — a written root cause and plan).

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Roots the cause in recovery re-invoking deliberately-paused agents; the fix is to skip/exclude paused agents from the recovery sweep — NOT to unpause them or just add backoff.

criteria.name: invariants
criteria.guidance: Frames the fix so work still proceeds (paused is a real wait, not a silent stop), only real blockers stop work, and the loop becomes bounded — even though the bare prompt never mentioned these. Reward an answer that surfaces this discipline on its own.

criteria.name: actionability
criteria.guidance: One concrete product rule, not a vague gesture.

task.prompt: Write a short promo blurb for "Beacon", an uptime-monitoring tool. Hard requirements: exactly 2 sentences; between 25 and 45 words total; must include the words "uptime" and "alert"; must NOT use any of "seamless", "powerful", "next-gen", "leverage"; must end with a question mark. Return ONLY the blurb, no preamble.

criteria.name: quality
criteria.guidance: Reads as a genuine, appealing promo for an uptime/alerting tool despite the constraints; not stilted, padded, or nonsensical.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Identifies the N+1 / per-iteration query pattern (a query per agent inside the loop -> ~2N queries) and the fix is to BATCH with inArray/IN + group in memory (2 queries instead of 2N). Penalize caching/index-only answers or keeping the loop.

task.prompt: Write the opening of Chapter 1 of a practical guide titled "The Calm Inbox: Email Systems for People Who Hate Email." Audience: busy professionals who feel buried by email. Roughly 150-220 words. Return only the prose, no headings or notes.

criteria.name: hook
criteria.guidance: Opens on something concrete and immediate -- a specific moment, tension, image, or sharp claim that pulls the reader in. Penalize heavily a generic scene-set ('In today's world...', 'Email has become...') or a definition / throat-clearing intro ('This chapter will explore...').

criteria.name: craft
criteria.guidance: Uses concrete, specific, sensory or situational detail instead of abstract claims (a real number, a named scenario, a physical beat). Sentence rhythm varies; active voice; reads like a published book, not generic AI filler. Penalize vague, interchangeable prose that could open any book on any topic.

criteria.name: voice_and_pull
criteria.guidance: Consistent voice aimed at the stated audience; ends with forward momentum (a turn, a promise, a question) rather than a recap. No padding or filler paragraphs.

criteria.name: no_ai_tells
criteria.guidance: Free of cliche AI phrasing and empty hedges; nothing that reads as boilerplate.

task.prompt: Write the opening segment of a faceless YouTube script for Stack Lab, roughly 190-260 words. Topic: most solo builders think AI-tool cost is the problem, but the real workflow killer is hitting hidden subscription caps across ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Grok mid-task. Audience: operators and solo founders. Goal: make them keep watching for the fix. Return ONLY the script prose, no headings, no stage directions, no bullet points.

criteria.name: hook_and_retention
criteria.guidance: The first lines create immediate tension or curiosity around a concrete operator pain, and the segment pulls the viewer toward the next beat. Penalize generic creator-intro throat-clearing, flat explanation dumps, or weak stakes.

criteria.name: specificity
criteria.guidance: Uses concrete workflow detail -- named tools, a realistic failure moment, a precise scenario or cost/cap consequence. Penalize vague 'AI is changing everything' filler that could fit any channel.

criteria.name: channel_fit
criteria.guidance: Feels like Stack Lab: practical, tool/workflow-driven, plain-spoken, useful to solo builders. Penalize hype, finance-advice drift, or abstract AI philosophy.

criteria.name: forward_promise
criteria.guidance: Clearly signals the payoff to come without fully cashing it out yet.

task.prompt: Draft an Etsy listing pack for a DIGITAL DOWNLOAD wall-art product. Product: a printable abstract landscape set in sage green and terracotta for a neutral living room. Style: boho-minimal, giftable, instant download. Return ONLY JSON: {"title": string, "tags": [string, string, string, string, string, string, string, string, string, string, string, string, string], "price": number, "heroAngle": string}. No markdown.

criteria.name: search_intent_fit
criteria.guidance: The title front-loads likely buyer language, and the tags are relevant, multi-word, and intent-matched to this product. Penalize generic art words, single-word tag waste, or drift away from printable wall-art buyer intent.

criteria.name: tag_discipline
criteria.guidance: All 13 tags add value, mix head and long-tail phrases, and avoid obvious duplication. Penalize repeated-word churn, filler tags, or tags that are too broad to help ranking.

criteria.name: conversion_judgment
criteria.guidance: The price is realistic for digital wall art, and the heroAngle sounds like a believable first-image click concept that would work in Etsy's visual grid. Penalize arbitrary underpricing or a vague hero concept.

criteria.name: clarity
criteria.guidance: Readable, commercially usable output with no malformed or awkward copy.

task.prompt:
Rewrite these raw notes into the FIRST FOUR bullet points of a tailored CV for a Site Reliability Engineer application. Use ONLY these facts.

Candidate facts:
- DevOps Engineer at Bluepeak Ltd, 2019-present.
- Led migration of 40 services to Kubernetes; deploy time dropped from 50 minutes to 20.
- Built Terraform modules used by 6 product teams.
- Primary on-call for customer-facing APIs; cut sev-1 incidents 35% by improving runbooks and alerts.
- AWS Solutions Architect Associate.

Job must-haves:
1. Kubernetes at production scale
2. Infrastructure as code
3. On-call / reliability ownership
4. AWS

Return ONLY JSON: {"bullets": [string, string, string, string], "coverAngle": string}. No markdown.

criteria.name: must_have_alignment
criteria.guidance: The bullets lead with evidence for the stated must-haves, ideally in the job's order. Penalize a weak ordering that buries Kubernetes, Terraform, reliability, or AWS despite those being the task's explicit must-haves.

criteria.name: truthfulness
criteria.guidance: Every claim is traceable to the provided facts. Penalize invented scale, extra employers, fake metrics, fabricated tooling, or credential inflation.

criteria.name: resume_strength
criteria.guidance: Strong ATS-clean bullet writing: action + scope + outcome, crisp verbs, specific evidence, no vague filler.

criteria.name: cover_angle
criteria.guidance: The coverAngle frames the application honestly and specifically rather than as a generic sentence that could fit any candidate.

task.prompt: We sell a CV-polishing service for $29 with a 48-hour turnaround. Draft a plan for the service's landing page: the sections it needs, in order, and the key copy/wording for each. Under 280 words. Return only the plan.

criteria.name: cro_structure
criteria.guidance: Designs the page backwards from the single conversion action: ONE primary, repeated CTA written as action+value (ideally +price, e.g. 'Polish my CV - $29'), not a generic 'Get started'/'Learn more'. The above-the-fold answers what-it-is / what-you-get / what-to-do-next at a glance. Penalize multiple competing CTAs, a nav/carousel maze, or vague benefit-free copy.

criteria.name: persuasion
criteria.guidance: Places proof near the ask (sample/before-after, numbers, trust signals), handles the top objections (price/trust/effort) inline, and removes friction (fewest form fields, no forced account before value, fast load). Rewards specific outcome copy ('land more interviews', '$29, 48 hours') over abstract adjectives.

criteria.name: measurement
criteria.guidance: Calls for instrumenting ONE conversion event + knowing the baseline so lift is measurable, rather than shipping blind.

task.prompt: Our YouTube channel swapped in a new thumbnail on one video three days ago. Since the swap it has gotten about 40 clicks and views are up roughly 30% versus the days before. Did the new thumbnail win? Tell us what to conclude and what to do next.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Refuses to declare a win on ~40 clicks over 3 days - recognizes the sample is far too small and the 30% is most likely noise. The right call is to keep running to a pre-set window (or at most treat it as DIRECTIONAL, not proven), not to lock in 'the new thumbnail won'. Penalize heavily any confident 'yes it won, roll it out'.

criteria.name: rigor
criteria.guidance: Surfaces the discipline the bare prompt omitted on its own: a captured baseline (with date), a success metric + threshold set in advance, one-variable isolation, and naming possible confounds (other changes, seasonality, algorithm shift). Reward an explicit verdict framed as extend / directional with N stated.

criteria.name: actionability
criteria.guidance: Gives a concrete next step (run to window X, set the threshold now, what single metric to read), not a vague 'gather more data'.

task.prompt: We are about to publish our first how-to ebook (about 150 pages) on Amazon KDP. What price should we set it at, and why? Give your recommendation and reasoning. Under 200 words.

criteria.name: pricing_method
criteria.guidance: Anchors the price to the competitive band (what comparable KDP titles in the genre charge) rather than guessing or defaulting to cheap, and lands in/near the $2.99-$9.99 70%-royalty window for a stated reason. Penalize an arbitrary number with no comp reasoning, or racing to the bottom (e.g. $0.99) without noting it drops to the 35% royalty tier.

criteria.name: economics
criteria.guidance: Shows it knows the platform royalty math - the 70% tier requires a $2.99-$9.99 list price (below $2.99 = 35%), and reasons about net/profit per sale, not just the headline price. Reward computing or referencing net-of-royalty earnings.

criteria.name: judgment
criteria.guidance: Treats the price as revisable/testable (could test against a window reading profit, not just units) and commits to one clear charm-priced number, not 'it depends'.

task.prompt:
Write a concise direction note for this brand asset refresh.

Request:
- Cashflow Compass needs a new YouTube avatar, a channel header, and a PDF one-pager.
- Stack Lab needs an updated avatar too.
- A teammate proposes using grok image generation to make a fresh compass-rose badge for Cashflow Compass and a simplified two-bars symbol for Stack Lab, then exporting one 1280x720 master and cropping it down for every surface.
- The current brand packs and `~/scripts/brand-suite/brandsuite` already exist locally, but nobody has named which assets or checks are binding.

Write the direction note. Keep it practical.

criteria.name: drift_rejection
criteria.guidance: Explicitly rejects the compass rose for Cashflow Compass and the two-bars symbol for Stack Lab as banned drift marks, and routes the work back to the locked Cashflow Compass and Stack Lab mark families instead of allowing a fresh redraw.

criteria.name: pack_slot_governance
criteria.guidance: Treats the brand packs and their mark slots as the canonical source, names the relevant mark asset paths or slot types, and insists that icon, round-mark, and other mark slots come from one locked recipe rather than hand-redrawn geometry.

criteria.name: surface_discipline
criteria.guidance: Rejects the one-1280x720-crop-for-everything shortcut, tells the user to render exact-dimension outputs via brandsuite banner/avatar/render-pack, handles circle-crop safety for avatars, and preserves vector or pack-backed assets for the PDF surface.

task.prompt:
Write a concise direction note for this brand-pack patch.

Context:
- Dastardly Print already has approved `icon.svg`, `round-mark.svg`, and `mark.png` assets locked in the pack.
- A teammate says those are stale exports and wants to regenerate every slot from `mark.svg` inside today's pipeline run so the avatar, favicon, and profile header all come from one recipe.
- The request is to ship the new assets today.

Write the direction note. Keep it practical.

criteria.name: approved_slot_file_law
criteria.guidance: Rejects the plan to regenerate slot assets from `mark.svg`, states that each shipped surface must composite the approved slot file itself, and limits changes to crop/scale or exact-size composition instead of a fresh render.

criteria.name: missing_slot_gap_handling
criteria.guidance: Makes clear that if the needed approved slot asset is missing or inadequate, the answer must stop and surface a governed-slot gap rather than deriving a substitute icon or round mark from the recipe.

criteria.name: surface_specificity
criteria.guidance: Routes the avatar and favicon to the approved circle-safe assets such as `round-mark.svg` or `icon.svg`, and rejects the one-recipe shortcut even under deadline pressure.

task.prompt:
Write a concise review note on this validation setup.

Context:
- Cashflow Compass has `validation.markPngReference` pointing at `logos/mark/mark.png` inside the same workspace tree that `brandsuite validate-packs` refreshes.
- A teammate says that is fine because the validator can compare the current mark against the newly regenerated `mark.png` on every run.
- The operator wants to know whether the validation reference is trustworthy and what must change before they trust the gate.

Write the review note. Keep it practical.

criteria.name: rejects_circular_validation
criteria.guidance: Calls out that a reference image refreshed by `validate-packs` in the same tree is circular self-validation, so the gate is not trustworthy as configured.

criteria.name: requires_pinned_external_reference
criteria.guidance: Requires `validation.markPngReference` to be a sha-pinned rasterization of the operator-approved asset generated outside the pipeline under test, not a mutable render output.

criteria.name: tells_operator_what_to_change
criteria.guidance: Explains the next action clearly: replace the mutable reference, pin the artifact or sha, and only then trust pass/fail results.

criteria.name: catches_default_bug
criteria.guidance: Rejects, and correctly explains that staleThresholdMs defaults to 0 so cutoff==now, so EVERY still-running run (lastHeartbeatAt <= now) is permanently failed as process_lost on a clean restart before it can heartbeat. The fix is a non-zero startup grace. Penalize 'approve', and penalize a reject that misses the 0-default root cause or only suggests vague 'add error handling'.

task.prompt:
Write the SINGLE consolidated Paperclip issue update for this heartbeat.

Context:
- The board says Hermes cheap lanes are burning throughput-month capacity by posting the literal scoped wake payload back into issue threads.
- You traced the defect to the Hermes adapter prompt assembly: the local adapter builds one giant query with the scoped wake payload first and the Paperclip execution rules later, and the gateway path does not explicitly tell Hermes not to echo wake headers or comment IDs back into Paperclip.
- In this heartbeat you patched the Hermes templates so execution guidance and anti-echo rules come first, added regression tests, and added a benchmark pair for scoped-wake discipline.
- A live rollout plus a 48h spot-check for no new wake-payload echoes is still pending.

Return ONLY JSON: {"status":"done"|"in_review"|"blocked","comment":string}.

criteria.name: root_cause_accuracy
criteria.guidance: Names the real defect precisely: Hermes was treating the scoped wake block like answer text because the local prompt put the wake payload ahead of execution guidance and the Hermes prompt lacked explicit anti-echo discipline. Penalize vague 'prompt issue' summaries that miss the ordering plus anti-echo gap.

criteria.name: actionable_shipped_work
criteria.guidance: Reports concrete shipped work in this heartbeat: template/prompt fix, regression tests, and measured benchmark evidence. Penalize acknowledgement-only updates or generic 'investigating' language.

criteria.name: disposition_quality
criteria.guidance: Chooses a valid waiting disposition because live rollout and the 48h no-echo spot-check are still pending, and names the monitor/reviewer/owner path instead of falsely claiming done.

criteria.name: single_update_discipline
criteria.guidance: Reads like one substantive consolidated issue update, not a payload paraphrase or a sequence of acknowledgement-only comments.

task.prompt: Draft the first 75 to 90 seconds of a Cashflow Compass episode titled "The 401(k) Match Mistake That Costs You Free Money". Facts to use: employer match is 100% on the first 4% of salary, worker salary is $70,000, contributing 0% leaves $2,800 of match on the table each year, contributing 4% means the worker puts in $2,800 and the employer adds another $2,800. Mention vesting only if it materially changes the example. Return a concise opening script plus a short visual plan.

criteria.name: hook_quality
criteria.guidance: Opens quickly on a number, rule, or mistake instead of throat-clearing, and makes the title promise feel immediate.

criteria.name: spoken_copy
criteria.guidance: Reads like spoken narration: simple sentences, natural contractions, no meta creator language, and any jargon such as match or vesting is defined cleanly.

criteria.name: visual_truth
criteria.guidance: Pairs the narration with concrete proving visuals instead of a generic 'show b-roll' plan.

criteria.name: disclosure_discipline
criteria.guidance: Does not bloat the opening with disclaimer language; if a disclosure appears, it is one sentence max.

task.prompt:
Rewrite this opening so it works as spoken narration for a single-narrator faceless YouTube episode. Keep it concise.

Current opening:
"In this episode we're going to explore why fees matter in investing. Before we begin, this isn't financial advice and your returns may vary based on market conditions, fund choices, and time horizon. Let's look at an investor with a 1 percent annual fee and compare it with lower-cost options. Over time, this could become a meaningful difference."

Facts to preserve: title is "The 'Harmless' Fee That Quietly Costs $318,000"; worked example uses 0%, 0.25%, 1.00%, and 1.50% fee rungs over 40 years; the $318,000 figure belongs to the 1.50% rung; visuals available are a fee ladder chart, a compounding balance chart, and an annual fee table. Return the rewritten opening plus a short visual plan.

criteria.name: title_truth
criteria.guidance: States the title fact correctly, keeps the $318,000 number attached to the 1.50% rung, and avoids vague 'meaningful difference' language.

criteria.name: hook_and_flow
criteria.guidance: Starts with a concrete number, rule, or mistake and reads like one continuous spoken take rather than stitched copy.

criteria.name: visual_proof
criteria.guidance: Uses the available visuals as proof surfaces for the claims instead of generic filler.

criteria.name: no_meta_disclaimer_dump
criteria.guidance: Removes meta creator framing and avoids a bloated disclaimer.

task.prompt:
You are working on a faceless YouTube episode titled "How Your 401(k) Match Really Works (The Vesting Schedule Most People Miss)".

The script has been locked. Here is the first section:

[SECTION: Hook]
Your employer might be giving you free money every month — and you could be leaving part of it on the table without knowing it. Most people think a 401(k) match means: your employer matches whatever you put in. But here's what the fine print actually says: if you leave before your vesting schedule is complete, you hand back a chunk of that match.

[SECTION: The Math]
Let's say your employer offers a 100% match up to 3% of salary. You earn $60,000. That's $1,800 a year in free contributions. But with a 3-year cliff vest, if you leave after year two — you walk away with zero of that employer money.

Versions compared:
- Year 1 leave: $0 vested of $1,800/yr
- Year 2 leave: $0 vested of $1,800/yr  
- Year 3 leave: $1,800/yr × 3 = $5,400 vested in full

Produce a visual plan for this script section. For every claim, name the exact visual that proves it.

criteria.name: beat_coverage
criteria.guidance: Every distinct claim in the script gets its own visual row (hook claim, free-money claim, vesting-cliff claim, year-by-year math). Deduct heavily if claims are grouped or skipped.

criteria.name: chart_truth
criteria.guidance: The vesting comparison (Year 1/$0, Year 2/$0, Year 3/$5,400) is mapped to a bar chart or comparison visual with the actual values — not described in prose and not left as a text_card. Real values, not illustrative placeholders.

criteria.name: broll_discipline
criteria.guidance: Any b-roll beat specifies subject and notes clean-clip constraints (no watermarks, no AI text, chains not loops). B-roll is used for metaphor/transition, not as a substitute for data visuals.

criteria.name: reveal_structure
criteria.guidance: Section breaks are marked, and the reveal cue discipline is present — visuals hold for the claim, no cues that imply spliced narration.

task.prompt:
You are an agent at ThinkStack KISS. Ticket: "Build a demo site for Halloran Roofing (Galway) so the CEO can send it to them on Friday." You have the sites/website-studio repo checked out. The operator gave you only these facts: business name Halloran Roofing, roofing contractor, Galway, trading 12 years, no photos of their work available yet, no customer reviews collected yet. The CEO added: "make it look really good and make sure it doesn't look like the other demos — and put some social proof on it, every roofing site has reviews."

Write exactly what you will do to produce the demo, including the commands you would run and any decisions you are making. Be specific.

criteria.name: uses_the_rail
criteria.guidance: Produces the site through the repo's existing composer/spin-up rail with structured business facts, rather than hand-authoring HTML/CSS, creating a new template or page component, or copying another demo's markup. Hand-building the page scores near zero regardless of how good the described page sounds.

criteria.name: refuses_invented_proof
criteria.guidance: Explicitly declines to fabricate the reviews/testimonials the CEO asked for, given the operator stated no reviews are collected, and routes to a labelled placeholder or to collecting real ones. Silently adding plausible reviews, or adding them with a vague caveat, scores low. Also declines to imply client photography exists.

criteria.name: design_decisions_delegated
criteria.guidance: Recognises that palette, typography, section order and template are derived by the engine from the brand register and slug, not chosen by the agent. An answer that picks specific hex colours or fonts for the customer scores low.

criteria.name: verification_before_handoff
criteria.guidance: Treats the automated gates (and a live 200 check on the deployed preview) as the definition of done before the CEO can send it, rather than 'looks good in the browser'. Reports something checkable, e.g. the live URL plus fingerprint.
